paragraphs.text: Founded in 2009 by coastal ecologist Dr. Renata Solís following the catastrophic erosion of the Nueces Bay shoreline, Brackwater Marsh Conservancy is a Gulf Coast nonprofit dedicated to restoring tidal wetlands and the communities that depend on them. We work where freshwater meets the sea — the brackish, breathing frontier of the continent.
Dr. Renata Solís — Founder & Executive Director. Marine ecologist, Texas A&M-Corpus Christi alumna, and lifelong advocate for the brackish ecotone.

Tidal wetlands are the most productive ecosystems on Earth, exchanging nutrients, sediment, and life between river and sea twice each day. Along the Gulf Coast, they shelter juvenile shrimp and redfish, anchor sediment against storm surge, and sequester carbon at rates that outpace tropical forests.

Yet for every acre we protect, another is lost to subsidence, salinization, or development. Brackwater Marsh Conservancy exists to reverse that math — one restored bayou, one replanted Spartina plug, one community partnership at a time.

The Texas and Louisiana coasts are subsiding while seas rise — a double squeeze that pushes salt water into freshwater swamps and drowns marshes faster than they can accrete. Hurricanes Harvey, Ida, and Beryl each rewrote the shoreline in ways that took decades to occur naturally.

Our restoration work is calibrated to this acceleration. We plant for the marsh of 2055, not 1985, building elevation gradients and freshwater refugia that give wildlife room to migrate upslope as the Gulf encroaches.

Brackwater's work rests on three interlocking pillars. Each reinforces the others: restored marsh becomes a living classroom; educated citizens become informed advocates; advocacy unlocks the public funding and policy that scales restoration.

We refuse to treat any single pillar as sufficient. A planted shoreline without community ownership erodes. A trained student without habitat to study disengages. A policy without science behind it collapses.
Replanting native grasses, removing invasives, and reopening tidal flow on the ground, parcel by parcel.
K–12 curriculum, field labs, and adult workshops that turn estuary literacy into civic action.
Coalition work with municipal, state, and tribal partners to defend wetland law and funding.
Our 2024 operating plan concentrates resources on three high-leverage initiatives identified by the board last November.
A Gulf Coast nonprofit restoring tidal wetlands since 2009. Corpus Christi, Texas.

visibleText: Brackwater Marsh Conservancy Mission Wetlands Volunteer Grants Contact Our Mission · Est. 2009 Restoring the Gulf's Living Edge Founded in 2009 by coastal ecologist Dr. Renata Solís following the catastrophic erosion of the Nueces Bay shoreline, Brackwater Marsh Conservancy is a Gulf Coast nonprofit dedicated to restoring tidal wetlands and the communities that depend on them. We work where freshwater meets the sea — the brackish, breathing frontier of the continent. RS Dr. Renata Solís — Founder & Executive Director. Marine ecologist, Texas A&M-Corpus Christi alumna, and lifelong advocate for the brackish ecotone. By the Numbers Fifteen Years of Coastal Stewardship 14,200 Acres Restored 38 Partner Organizations $2.1M Distributed in Grants Mission Statement Why Tidal Wetlands Matter The Pulse of the Estuary Tidal wetlands are the most productive ecosystems on Earth, exchanging nutrients, sediment, and life between river and sea twice each day. Along the Gulf Coast, they shelter juvenile shrimp and redfish, anchor sediment against storm surge, and sequester carbon at rates that outpace tropical forests. Yet for every acre we protect, another is lost to subsidence, salinization, or development. Brackwater Marsh Conservancy exists to reverse that math — one restored bayou, one replanted Spartina plug, one community partnership at a time. Ecological Snapshot Region Western Gulf of Mexico Range Brownsville, TX to Pensacola, FL Habitats Mangrove, salt marsh, brackish, cypress Indicator Species Roseate spoonbill, diamondback terrapin Carbon Stored ~1.4M metric tons CO₂e The Rising Threat The Texas and Louisiana coasts are subsiding while seas rise — a double squeeze that pushes salt water into freshwater swamps and drowns marshes faster than they can accrete. Hurricanes Harvey, Ida, and Beryl each rewrote the shoreline in ways that took decades to occur naturally. Our restoration work is calibrated to this acceleration. We plant for the marsh of 2055, not 1985, building elevation gradients and freshwater refugia that give wildlife room to migrate upslope as the Gulf encroaches. Sea-Level Pressure Gulf SLR Rate ~6.5 mm/year Subsidence up to 12 mm/yr (Houston-Galveston) Marsh Loss ~25 sq mi / year (LA) Storm Surge Buffer 1 acre marsh = $8,200 / yr protection The Three-Pillar Strategy Brackwater's work rests on three interlocking pillars. Each reinforces the others: restored marsh becomes a living classroom; educated citizens become informed advocates; advocacy unlocks the public funding and policy that scales restoration. We refuse to treat any single pillar as sufficient. A planted shoreline without community ownership erodes. A trained student without habitat to study disengages. A policy without science behind it collapses. Restoration Replanting native grasses, removing invasives, and reopening tidal flow on the ground, parcel by parcel. Education K–12 curriculum, field labs, and adult workshops that turn estuary literacy into civic action. Advocacy Coalition work with municipal, state, and tribal partners to defend wetland law and funding. This Year's Focus Our 2024 operating plan concentrates resources on three high-leverage initiatives identified by the board last November. Aransas-Copano living shoreline expansion (320 acres) Estuary Education Initiative roll-out in 14 school districts Coastal Resilience Fellowship cohort #3 From the Founder "A marsh is not a place. It is a conversation between freshwater and salt, between mud and air, between us and what we choose to remember. Our job is to keep that conversation going." — Dr. Renata Solís, Founder Join the work. Whether you have an hour, a skill, or a dollar — there is a place for you on the marsh. Explore volunteer roles , our grant programs , or simply say hello . Get Involved Explore Our Wetlands Brackwater Marsh A Gulf Coast nonprofit restoring tidal wetlands since 2009. Corpus Christi, Texas.
